description: To 'lupaki' is to bend flexible material so one part lies over another, usually along a deliberate line. Cloth, paper, and soft coverings fold for keeping or use. The verb concerns the placement of layers rather than damage to the material.
articulatory_notes: Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for 'l', release air along its sides, and round into high-back 'u'. Close the lips for unvoiced 'p', then open them into stressed central 'a'. Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into final high-front 'i'.
sound_symbolism: Deep lateral 'lu' moves into the broad opening of stressed 'pa'. Final 'ki' shifts the closure from the lips to the back of the mouth and ends on a bright edge. The contour turns twice, a useful bodily mnemonic for material laid back over itself.
usage_notes: The verb directly takes the flexible material. Number, direction, and purpose can appear separately. Nominal use may refer to folding as an activity or to one resulting fold, according to context.

pillars.art-nouveau-aesthetics: A fold changes how one uncut surface catches light and shadow. Its line comes from the material's own movement.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Folded cloth fit a chest, protected a finished edge, and left the next morning's work easier to begin.

semantic_domains.activity: laying one part of flexible material over another
semantic_domains.creation: forming layers and a deliberate crease
